title: The Disproportionate-Assets Case: A Continuing Story! (6)
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the witness-by-witness inventory, company and property names, stated valuations, attributed allegations, numbered lists, and serialized ending. The source itself gives differing figures between the narrative and later lists, including 800 and 898 acres, 600 and 100 acres, and 80 and 30 vehicles; each is retained where printed rather than harmonised. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which only clear OCR character, numeral-separator, scan-marker, spacing, and word-break errors are repaired. The canonical source remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Bhavani Singh began his final arguments on 21 March 2014. In the Bengaluru case, Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh presented to the court, in sequence, what the witnesses had stated in their evidence. In Tamil Nadu, the details revealed through that testimony were published by no newspapers except *Murasoli* and *Dinakaran*. The evidence he read included the following.
He read testimony concerning the purchase for Jayalalithaa of 800 acres at Kodanadu, 1,190 acres in Tirunelveli, 200 acres at Uthukottai and 25 acres at Siruthavur.

Bhavani Singh said that the lands were purchased after Radhakrishnan, then an Agriculture Department officer in government service, had been improperly used for private purposes and made to inspect them.
He also explained to the judge the evidence given by those who had supplied vehicles.

Bhavani Singh pointed out during his arguments: “During the five years from July 1991 to April 1996, the assets accumulated by Jayalalithaa and the other accused were then worth many lakhs. Today their value has grown beyond Rs 4,000 crore.”

The Public Prosecutor further said: “Documents from several companies have been filed to establish that accused numbers one to four are connected with one another. The essence of the case is that the four accused entered into a conspiracy and, while the first accused was Chief Minister, used her authority to accumulate assets beyond their income for her and the other three accused associated with her.”

He also placed before the court details that “the first accused, Jayalalithaa, took the third accused, V.N. Sudhakaran, as her foster son and conducted his wedding on a grand scale,” and that “documents establish that the accused accumulated assets disproportionate to their income.”
Bhavani Singh read documents concerning the accused's acquisition of several companies and the purchase of many immovable properties in those companies' names.

He read the evidence of the owner of a Neelankarai bungalow visited and purchased by Sasikala and Ilavarasi; the testimony of the owner of a Neelankarai property with a bungalow and swimming pool purchased by Sudhakaran; the testimony of Agriculture Department officer Radhakrishnan concerning his being summoned to improve the 800-acre Kodanadu property purchased by Jayalalithaa; the same officer's evidence that Sudhakaran improperly sent him on a private assignment to inspect 1,190 acres in Tirunelveli before their purchase; and the evidence of land broker Rajaram, who approached the owners and arranged the purchase of 100 acres at Walajabad near Chennai.

Special Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh said: “There is no relationship whatsoever between the financial position of the accused and the lands they accumulated. The testimony clearly establishes that all these lands were purchased through assets accumulated beyond their income. Properties connected with the disproportionate-assets case, purchased for several lakhs during those five years, are today worth several thousand crores.”
Among the properties of Jayalalithaa listed in court by Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh were:

1. 600 acres at Walajabad near Chennai. 2. A bungalow on 25 acres at Siruthavur. 3. Two acres at Neelankarai. 4. 800 acres and bungalows at Kodanadu. One acre there may be worth Rs 5 crore. This alone crosses Rs 4,000 crore, though that is only an estimated value. 5. 200 acres at Kanchipuram. 6. 1,190 acres in the Meenankulam, Sivarankulam and Vellankulam areas of Kanyakumari. 7. 200 acres in the Srivaikuntam area of Thoothukudi district. 8. 100 acres in the name of Reverie Agro Farm. 9. Cars and trucks of many kinds in eighty colours. 10. A vineyard in Hyderabad.
The values of properties recorded in the testimony placed before the court by the Public Prosecutor over four days were listed as follows:

1. Jayalalithaa's side purchased 100 acres at Walajabad. The government value of the land was Rs 40 crore and its market value Rs 50 crore. 2. The land purchased at Siruthavur measured 25.4 acres. Its government value was Rs 42.5 crore and market value Rs 50 crore. 3. The two-acre Neelankarai property held by Jayalalithaa's side had a government value of Rs 70 crore and market value of Rs 100 crore. 4. The 200 acres purchased by Jayalalithaa's side at Kanchipuram had a government value of Rs 60 crore and market value of Rs 100 crore. 5. The 1,190 acres purchased by Jayalalithaa's side in Kanyakumari had a government value of Rs 175 crore and market value of Rs 292 crore. 6. The five acres purchased at Paiyanur had a government value of Rs 10 crore and market value of Rs 15 crore. 7. At Kodanadu, one acre had a government value of Rs 3 crore and market value of Rs 5 crore. The 898 acres purchased there by Jayalalithaa's side had a government value of Rs 2,450 crore and market value of Rs 4,500 crore. 8. 200 acres in the Srivaikuntam area of Thoothukudi district. 9. 100 acres in the name of Reverie Agro Farm. 10. Cars and trucks of many kinds in thirty colours. 11. Jayalalithaa's vineyard in Hyderabad.

According to this list, among the properties acquired while Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister during the five years from 1991 to 1996, one portion alone now had a government value of Rs 2,847.50 crore and a market value of Rs 5,107 crore. If jewellery, diamonds, company investments and other movable assets were added, several more crores would result. That is, within five years she had acquired assets 310 times greater than those she possessed before assuming office as Chief Minister.

Jayalalithaa's side created thirty-two new companies over two years, 1993 and 1994, for the purpose of purchasing and accumulating these assets. Crores of rupees in cash were deposited in bank accounts in the companies' names, and through those funds land worth several hundred crores was purchased and accumulated across Tamil Nadu in those names.

In particular, 200 acres were purchased in the name of Meadow Agro Farms; 1,190 acres in the name of Riverway Agro Products; 898 acres in the name of Kodanadu Estates; and 50 acres in the name of Ramaraj Agro Mills. Thirty-two companies were established one after another in names connected with Jayalalithaa's side. They included Super Duper Company; Anjaneya Printers, where the J.J. TV office and *Namadhu MGR* office were located; Jaya Publications; Sasi Enterprises; Indo-Doha Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals; Green Farm Houses; and Metal Kings, for which the TANSI land was purchased.

Apart from these, a witness named Ahmed testified that 2,400 square feet of land at Neelankarai on Chennai's Mamallapuram East Coast Road was purchased in the name of Ilavarasi, the fourth accused, on 17 May 1995.

V. Bhaskaran, then an Executive Engineer in the Tamil Nadu Public Works Department, testified that Ramaraj Rice Mill at Vandampalayam in Tiruvarur district was purchased and converted into Ramaraj Agro Mills.

Gopal Rathinam, who had served as auditor to the first accused Jayalalithaa, testified: “I was Jayalalithaa's auditor from 1964 to 1986. The assets held in the names of Jayalalithaa and her mother, and the income derived from them, were audited annually and income tax was paid. Income from the Kalanikethan drama troupe, the jackpot won through horse racing in 1970, and crops cultivated on the land was also shown in the asset accounts.”

Rajendran, who worked as a videographer for the Tamil Nadu Police, gave evidence that he filmed searches conducted by Tamil Nadu's Directorate of Vigilance and Anti-Corruption at properties belonging to the accused, including Jayalalithaa. These included Number 31A Poes Garden; the house at Number 36 Poes Garden; the Siruthavur bungalow; Paiyanur bungalow; Hyderabad vineyard; Kodaikanal bungalow; Kodanadu tea estate; and houses, bungalows and offices in Guindy, T. Nagar, Anna Nagar, Kottivakkam, Neelankarai, Injambakkam, Vettuvankeni, Arumbakkam, Parameswaran Nagar, Mandaiveli, Nungambakkam, Santhome and Cheyyur in Chennai, as well as *Namadhu MGR*, Anjaneya Printers, Jaya Publications, Sasi Enterprises and numerous other premises.

A witness named Tajudeen testified that his family had decided to sell a building they owned on Greams Road in Chennai. A person who learned of this insisted that the building be sold to them. Tajudeen executed the sale of the approximately 60,000-square-foot building in the names of Jayalalithaa, Sasikala and V.N. Sudhakaran. He testified that he received a cheque for Rs 1,84,500 and Rs 500 in cash. The building was being used by Sasi Enterprises.

Rajagopalan testified that the accused purchased land belonging to his family in Mannargudi, Thanjavur district, for Rs 8,85,000. He said the deed was registered using the address Number 36, Poes Garden, Chennai.

Venkataraman testified that he owned 1.5 acres at Siruthavur on Mamallapuram Road. Although he lived in Triplicane, he regularly visited the land. On one visit he found a thorn fence erected over part of it. When he questioned neighbours, they said three men had put it up. Those who erected the fence later met him and insisted that he sell the entire land to them.

He said that he had no intention of selling. But they threatened him: “The purchaser is Jayalalithaa, the Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu. Do not be obstinate; execute the deed.” Venkataraman, the landowner, said: “With no other choice, I conveyed land worth several lakhs for Rs 39,000.”

On the basis of the testimony described above, Public Prosecutor Bhavani Singh explained the methods allegedly followed by Jayalalithaa and the other accused and the many places in which they had purchased and accumulated property.
(To be continued)
With affection,
M.K.
